questions.text: Why is minimizing the Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss mathematically equivalent to maximizing the likelihood of network weights under Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)?
options: Because the Gaussian log-likelihood function contains a term proportional to the negative sum of squared differences, which directly corresponds to MSE when maximizing with respect to weights.
Because the derivative of the MSE loss function scales the learning rate by a factor of $\sigma^2$.
Because Gaussian noise forces the gradients to follow an orthogonal projection inside the Hilbert space.
Because the MSE loss function is derived from a Bernoulli probability model where labels are continuous.
questions.correct_answer: Because the Gaussian log-likelihood function contains a term proportional to the negative sum of squared differences, which directly corresponds to MSE when maximizing with respect to weights.
questions.explanation: Assuming target variables are subject to i.i.d. Gaussian noise $\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, the conditional probability density is Gaussian. The log-likelihood function is $\ln L(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$. Maximizing this function with respect to weights $\mathbf{w}$ requires minimizing the summation term $\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$, which is the numerator of the MSE loss function.

questions.text: Which of the following equations correctly expresses the relationship between Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE), Shannon Entropy of the target distribution $H(P)$, and the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between true and predicted distributions?
questions.explanation: By definition, the KL divergence is $D_{\text{KL}}(P \parallel \hat{P}) = \sum P(x) \ln P(x) - \sum P(x) \ln \hat{P}(x) = -H(P) + H(P, \hat{P})$. Since CCE is the cross-entropy $H(P, \hat{P})$, rearranging terms yields $\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}} = H(P) + D_{\text{KL}}(P \parallel \hat{P})$. Because the true labels $P(x)$ are fixed during training, the entropy $H(P)$ is a constant, making the minimization of CCE equivalent to minimizing the KL divergence.

questions.text: How do the optimization behaviors of Forward KL divergence ($D_{\text{KL}}(P \parallel Q)$) and Reverse KL divergence ($D_{\text{KL}}(Q \parallel P)$) differ when approximating a multi-modal true distribution $P$ using a unimodal distribution $Q$?
options: Forward KL is zero-avoiding (mean-seeking) and forces $Q$ to cover all modes of $P$, while Reverse KL is zero-forcing (mode-seeking) and forces $Q$ to focus on a single mode of $P$.
Forward KL is mode-seeking and focuses on a single peak, while Reverse KL forces $Q$ to be a uniform distribution.
Forward KL requires both distributions to be Gaussian, whereas Reverse KL allows discrete distributions.
Forward KL minimizes computational time by skipping the log calculation, whereas Reverse KL requires numerical integration.
questions.correct_answer: Forward KL is zero-avoiding (mean-seeking) and forces $Q$ to cover all modes of $P$, while Reverse KL is zero-forcing (mode-seeking) and forces $Q$ to focus on a single mode of $P$.
questions.explanation: In Forward KL ($D_{\text{KL}}(P \parallel Q) = \sum P(x) \ln(P(x)/Q(x))$), if $P(x) > 0$, we must have $Q(x) > 0$ to avoid infinite divergence, resulting in a zero-avoiding, mean-seeking fit that covers all modes. In Reverse KL ($D_{\text{KL}}(Q \parallel P) = \sum Q(x) \ln(Q(x)/P(x))$), if $P(x) = 0$, we must have $Q(x) = 0$ to avoid infinite divergence, resulting in a zero-forcing, mode-seeking fit that focuses on a single mode of $P$ and ignores others.
